segments.s398-g33: وزان جایگه ساربان را بخواند is built with وزان (a compound-like prepositional adverb 'and/from that place') + جایگه ('place') specifying the locus; ساربان را is the definite object marked by را; بخواند is a third-person singular past (poetic/archaic narrative) meaning 'called' or 'summoned' here. Word order is common Persian: locative phrase first, then object, then verb.
segments.s398-g34: به پیش پشوتن به زانو نشاند: به پیش پشوتن means 'in the presence of / before Peshotan' (به + پیش + proper name); the second به introduces the target position 'onto' (به زانو = 'onto the knee'). نشاند is an archaic/poetic past transitive meaning 'set/caused to sit' (3rd sg past). The two به phrases mark location/recipient and the place he is seated.
segments.s398-g35: بدو گفت صد بارکش سرخ‌موی: بدو ('to him') introduces the indirect object/person addressed; گفت here functions as the narrative past 'he said' (3rd sg past). صد بارکش is a descriptive epithet 'the porter of a hundred loads' (بارکش = beast/porter, modified by صد). سرخ‌موی is an adjective phrase 'red‑haired' modifying بارکش. The segment strings together the addressee, the speech verb, and the description of whom to bring.
segments.s398-g36: بیاور سرافراز با رنگ و بوی: بیاور is an imperative 'bring'; سرافراز is an epithet/adjunct meaning 'honored/proud' applied to the person/item to be brought. با رنگ و بوی ('with color and scent') uses با + two nouns linked by و to qualify how he should be presented. The imperative governs the rest as its object/descriptors.
segments.s398-g37: ازو ده شتر بار دینار کن: ازو ('from him/of him') marks the source; ده شتر ('ten camels') is the container/units to be laden; بار دینار کن is an imperative construction where بار (load) + دینار (dinar/money) + کن (imperative 'make/load') means 'load with dinars' or 'make the load be dinars.' The verb کن is the imperative that governs the command.
segments.s398-g38: دگر پنج دیبای چین بارکن: دگر پنج = 'another five' (dگر = another/other, followed by the numeral); دیبای چین = 'Chinese brocade' (دیبای brocade, چین qualifying as 'China/Chinese'); بارکن is the imperative 'load!'. The phrase gives a second command mirroring the previous imperative.
segments.s398-g39: دگر پنج هرگونه‌ای گوهران: دگر پنج = 'another five'; هرگونه‌ای گوهران is a noun phrase 'gems of every kind' (هرگونه‌ای = 'of any kind/various', گوهران = 'gems'). The segment lists additional treasure items; Persian allows the numeral and modifier to precede the noun cluster.
segments.s398-g40: یکی تخت زرین و تاج سران: یکی here means 'one' or 'a certain one' introducing items; تخت زرین = 'golden throne' (noun + adjective), و links to تاج سران = 'the crown of the chiefs/leaders' (سران = 'leaders/heads' serving as possessive/genitive notion). The whole nominal phrase enumerates high-status objects.
segments.s398-g89: This lists the goods being traded: 'هم' here means 'also / both' as the correlative beginning; 'از' marks the material/contents ('of' or 'from'); the nouns are coordinated by و (and). The sequence is a simple nominal series (گوهر، افسر، رنگ، بوی) that names what the speaker deals in.
segments.s398-g90: This is a correlative 'both...and' construction: فروشنده‌ام is a noun (فروشنده) plus the 1st‑person copular/enclitic -ام = 'I am a seller'; the following هم repeats 'also' and links to خریدار جوی. خریدار here is 'buyer', while جوی functions adjectivally (from جویا / جویدن sense) meaning 'seeking' or 'in search of' — so the phrase means 'and also a buyer, seeking (goods)'.
segments.s398-g91: The locative prepositional phrase 'به بیرون' ('to/outside') comes first, followed by the noun phrase 'دژ کاله' (a small fortress / citadel); the clause ends with the 1st‑person past verb بگذاشتم. بگذاشتم is classical: the prefix بـ (here part of the verbal form) plus past گـذاشتم, meaning 'I left / I placed / I set (down)'.
segments.s398-g92: This is a complete clause: جهان is the theme/subject ('the world'); در پناه تو is a prepositional phrase ('in/under your protection'); پنداشتم is the 1st‑person past of پنداشتن meaning 'I thought / I supposed / I imagined' and predicates the state of the world with respect to the addressee's protection.
segments.s398-g93: Conditional marker اگر ('if') opens the clause; شاه is the logical subject ('the king'); بیند is the classical present/subjunctive of دیدن often used for a potential/future event ('sees' / 'should see'); که introduces the subordinate clause 'that this caravan...' and این کاروان is the noun phrase object/topic of that subordinate clause.
segments.s398-g94: به دروازه دژ is a locative phrase ('to the gate of the fortress'); the verb کشد here is the classical present/finite form of کشیدن meaning 'to draw/drag/bring' (the sense list provided lacks this meaning, see note); ساروان (the caravan‑driver/leader) follows the verb — in poetry agents can appear after the verb, so the clause reads 'the caravan‑leader brings (it) to the fortress gate.'
segments.s398-g95: This is an optative/benedictional clause: 'به بخت تو' ('by/through your fortune') marks the protecting cause; 'از هر بد' ('from every ill') is the source/thing to be avoided; ایمن شوم uses the subjunctive شوم (1st person) meaning 'may I become / may I be secure' — a wish for safety is expressed.
segments.s398-g96: بدین is the classical contraction of به + این ('by/with this' or 'in this'); سایه مهر تو is a genitive group 'the shade (protection) of your affection/favour'; بغنوم is an archaic/poetic 1st‑person optative of غنود (given lemma غنود#غنو) meaning 'may I slumber / may I sleep' or 'may I find rest' — so the whole clause expresses a wish to rest under the beloved's protective affection.
segments.s398-g161: The phrase places the definite object 'درِ کلبه' ('the door of the cottage') up front with the object marker 'را' (را marks a definite direct object). 'نامور' (here a personal name/epithet 'Namvūr', literally 'renowned') is the subject that follows the object by poetic inversion, and 'باز کرد' is the compound verb 'باز کردن' (to open) split as adjective/stem + auxiliary ('باز' + 'کرد') giving the past action 'opened.'
segments.s398-g162: 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from' indicating source or relation ('from the merchants'). 'دژ پرآواز' is a noun ('fortress') followed by a postpositive adjective 'پرآواز' ('loud‑voiced, resounding/celebrated'), and 'کرد' is the verb 'to make/do' used causatively — together meaning 'he caused the merchants' fortress to be resounding' (i.e., he made the fortress resound).
segments.s398-g163: 'همی' is a habitual/continuous adverb ('still/kept on'); 'بود' is the past copula/auxiliary ('was') forming a progressive/habitual sense; 'چندی' here means 'for a while'; and 'خرید و فروخت' are nominalized or activity forms 'buying and selling' (the two actions joined by 'و'). Together they mean 'he was for a while engaged in buying and selling.'
segments.s398-g164: 'همی' again gives continuity; 'هرکسی' means 'each person/everyone'; 'چشم خود را' ('his own eye') is the definite object (را marks it); and 'بدوخت' is the past verb 'sewed' used figuratively here — i.e., 'sewed his eye (to it)' = 'kept his gaze fixed' on whatever was happening. The clause therefore means 'everyone kept their eyes fixed on him/it.'
segments.s398-g165: 'ز' marks source ('from the wealthy'); 'دینارگان' means 'the moneyed/wealthy ones'; 'یک درم' is the quantity 'one dirham'; and 'بستدی' is a past form 'bastadī' meaning 'he kept/closed up/put aside' — here 'he kept one dirham (from them).' The word order is source → noun phrase → verb, typical of classical narrative.
segments.s398-g166: 'همی' again signals habitual or continuing action. The pair 'این ... آن ...' ('this ... that ...') with the directional/relational elements 'بران' and 'برین' (poetic/archaic forms meaning roughly 'from/on this side' and 'from/on that side') creates the idiomatic sense 'from this and from that.' 'برزدی' is an archaic past verb here meaning roughly 'took' or 'skimmed' (used elliptically), so the whole line says he took a little from here and a little from there.
segments.s398-g73: This noun phrase identifies the object: 'یکی' is the indefinite numeral/article ('a certain' / 'a single') placed before the head noun 'طاس' (cup). 'پر' is a postpositive adjective meaning 'full (of)' and governs the following noun 'گوهر' (gems). 'شاهوار' is an adjectival epithet ('regal, majestic') that further qualifies the cup; the whole phrase is a compact, poetic noun cluster.
segments.s398-g74: This is a short prepositional/advantage phrase giving source or value: 'ز' before 'دینار' means 'from/of (dinar)' indicating cost or material. 'چندی' quantifies ("several/some"). The second 'ز' in 'ز بهر نثار' is causal/teleological ('for, for the sake of'), and 'بهر نثار' means 'for offering/dedication' (purpose). The sequence states that the cup involved several dinars intended as an offering.
segments.s398-g75: 'که' is a relative/complementizer linking back to the preceding noun (the cup): here it means 'upon which' or 'on which'. 'بر' is the preposition 'on/upon'. 'تافتش' is the verb 'shone' with the enclitic object '-ش' (referring to the cup) — note the poetic order where the verb precedes the nouns that act as its logical subject/causers ('ساعد و آستین' = 'forearm and sleeve').
segments.s398-g76: A list of additional gifts/items in simple SVO-free noun phrases: 'یکی اسپ' = 'one horse/steed' (numeral before noun), then 'و' connects 'دو جامه دیبای چین' = 'two garments (robes) of brocade with pleats/folds' where 'دیبای' specifies the fabric and 'چین' (here 'folds/pleats') qualifies the garments.
segments.s398-g77: 'بران' = 'on/over that' is a locative adverbial referring back to the cup. 'طاس' is repeated as the noun being described. 'پوشیده‌تایی' is a poetic/compound expression describing coveredness or a covering (a participial/nominal idea of being wrapped), and 'حریر' names the material (silk). Together they say the cup was covered with silk.
segments.s398-g78: This continues the description: 'حریر' (silk) is reiterated; 'از بر و زیر' literally 'from/top and under' i.e. 'both on the outside and the inside'; then 'مشک و عبیر' list the scents placed under/within the covering. The preposition 'از' here marks the distribution/arrangement ('silk — on top and beneath').
segments.s398-g79: 'به نزدیک' is an adverbial phrase meaning 'near' or 'to the vicinity of.' The verb 'شد' (lit. 'became' / 'came to be') takes the predicative noun 'چاره‌جوی' ('seeker of remedy' / 'seeker of counsel') — idiomatically 'he came (near) Arjasp seeking counsel.' The subject is omitted (understood from context); Persian often omits pronouns when clear.
segments.s398-g80: 'به دیبا بیاراسته' is a prepositional/participial phrase describing his dress: 'به' with the fabric 'دیبا' means 'in/with brocade,' and 'بیاراسته' (here used in the sense 'adorned/arrayed') qualifies his appearance. 'رنگ و بوی' ('color and scent') further describe his outward look and fragrance; together they portray him as richly clothed and perfumed. (Note: poetic diction and inverted order are normal here.)
segments.s398-g145: بدو is a prepositional/pronominal phrase meaning 'to him/unto him' placed before the verb for emphasis. گفت is the past tense verb 'said', introducing reported speech. از کار اسفندیار is a prepositional phrase where از governs 'کار' ('deed/affair') and links it to the proper name اسفندیار as its possessor/object.
segments.s398-g146: به ایران is a locative/recipient phrase 'to/for Iran' marking where the news had reached or concerned. خبر بود is an existential/past construction: خبر 'news' + بود (past of بودن) 'there was'. وز here is an archaic connective/particle introducing further information (not the modern sense 'gust' listed in the lexicon), and گرگسار is a place-name or adjective 'wolf‑infested' that completes that particle.
segments.s398-g147: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed poetic ordering: چنین 'thus' + داد (gave) + پاسخ (answer) = 'thus gave answer' (i.e. 'thus he replied'). که‌ای introduces the quoted or vocative address: که (that/that is) + ای (vocative) here functions to introduce direct speech or an exclamation, and نیک‌خوی is the vocative adjective 'kind‑natured' addressing someone.
segments.s398-g148: سخن راند is a poetically phrased verb: سخن 'speech/words' + راند (past of راندن 'to drive') = 'drove forth words', i.e. 'uttered/spoke'. زین here is an archaism (in context = 'from this' or 'by this'); هر کسی 'every person' follows and بارزوی (ب- + ارزو/رَزو) functions adverbially 'with desire / in hope', describing the effect of the speech.
segments.s398-g149: یکی گفت is the narrative formula 'someone said' (یکی 'someone/a certain one' + گفت 'said'). کاسفندیار is the proper name in the reported clause, and از پدر is a genitive/agent phrase 'toward/from his father'—از here marks the relation (concerning or against the father).
segments.s398-g150: پرآزار گشت is 'became very troublesome/cruel': پرآزار is an intensive adjective 'full of harm' and گشت is past of گشتن 'became/turned (into)'. و is the coordinator 'and'. بپیچید سر is a compound action: بپیچید (past, reflexive/intransitive 'turned') + سر 'head' — together 'turned his head (aside)' or 'bent/averted his head.'
segments.s398-g151: دگر گفت is 'another said' (دگر 'another/other' + گفت 'said'). کو here is an archaic particle used in reported speech ('that/which/where' in older usage) introducing the clause that follows. از دژ گنبدان is a prepositional phrase 'from the fortress of Gonbedan' where دژ is 'fortress' and گنبدان is a place-name or adjective functioning as its identifier.
segments.s398-g152: سپه برد here means he 'took/led an army' — سپه 'army/host' followed by برد (classical past of بردن 'to carry/take'); the supplied lexicon sense 'victory' does not match the verbal use, so برد is used as a verb in context. و is 'and'. شد بر ره is a verbal phrase 'set out on the road' (شد here functions as 'went/became' in the sense of 'began/was on' the way), بر ره = 'on the road/onto the path', and هفتخوان names the destination 'the Seven Trials' (a famous route of seven passes/trials).
segments.s398-g25: چنین دان is an imperative construction: چنین (thus) + دان (imperative 'consider/know'), i.e. 'consider/know thus'. که introduces a subordinate clause ('that ...'). آن کار کرد is a simple past clause ('that deed did/was done'), and منست is an archaic predicative form (here functioning as an adjectival predicate meaning 'is well‑arranged / fitting') that closes the clause.
segments.s398-g26: نه negates the following phrase ('not'). از چاره is a prepositional phrase 'from/of a device/expedient' (چاره = contrivance/means). هم‌نبرد is a compound noun 'comrade‑in‑arms / fellow warrior'. The final منست repeats the predicative adjective 'is fitting/well‑arranged' and links back to the prior clause (i.e. 'it is not from the contrivance of a comrade').
segments.s398-g27: سپه را uses را as the definite object marker (object = 'the army'). بیارای is an imperative verb ('array/prepare/put in order' — here commanding preparation). و coordinates the next command. ز is the poetic preposition 'from' and ایدر is an adverb 'here'; بران functions here as a verbal/adverbial element meaning 'send/drive forth/from that place' in the coordinated command.
segments.s398-g28: This is a coordinated nominal phrase giving instruments/companions to bring: زره‌دار is 'armored warrior(s)' (the men to bring). با + خود means 'with (their) own' or 'bring with you'. و connects the second item (گرز گران), where گرز is the noun 'mace' and گران is an adjective 'heavy/weighty' qualifying it.
segments.s398-g29: درفش من is a possessive noun phrase 'my standard' (من here marks possession). از دور is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'from a distance / at a distance'. بر + پای is a locative expression literally 'upon the base/foot' (i.e. set up on the ground). کن is the imperative 'do/put' commanding the action.
segments.s398-g30: سپه را again uses را to mark the object 'the army'. به قلب اندرون is a prepositional phrase 'into the heart/center within' (قلب = heart/core, اندرون = inside). جای کن is an imperative 'place/position (it)', ordering that the army be located in that inner position.
segments.s398-g31: بران here functions as an adverbial/locative 'thereupon/there' or a directional marker tied to the previous placement. تیز is an adverb/adjective 'swift/keen', describing the manner. با + گرزه indicates instrument ('with a mace'), and گاوسار is used as a name/epithet attached to the mace (i.e. 'Gausar's mace' or 'the mace of Gausar').
segments.s398-g32: چنان کن is an imperative 'do so / make it so'. که introduces the result clause 'so that ...'. خوانندت is a 3rd person plural present-subjunctive form + object clitic 'ت' meaning 'they call you' (literally 'may they call you'), and اسفندیار is the name being invoked as the desired reputation or title.
segments.s398-g137: به X گفت is the common periphrastic pattern 'said to X' (به = 'to'). ای introduces a vocative ('O'). زاد is a postpositive noun meaning 'born (of)' and here forms a compound with the preceding token (رد زاد = 'Rad‑born'); مرد is the head noun 'man'. Word order is vocative/address after the reporting verb: 'said to Kharad, “O Rad‑born man...”.
segments.s398-g138: به رنجی is a prepositional phrase 'in/at suffering' (به + noun with -ی as indefinite/index). همی is the older continuous/aspect particle (marks ongoing or habitual action) and is used with گرد here; گرد is an older verb 'to go/become/turn' (used intransitively). پوزش مگرد is a negative prohibitive: مگرد functions as 'do not' + verb, so 'پوزش مگرد' = 'do not make/ask for pardon'. The clause means roughly 'when in suffering, do not go about asking pardon.'
segments.s398-g139: ز دربان marks source/agent ('from the doorkeeper' or 'by the doorkeeper'). نباید is the impersonal/modal negative 'ought not / should not'. The clitic -ت on نباید (written here as ت) indicates the 2nd‑person object 'you' (you ought not). را is the definite/direct‑object marker attached after that clitic. بار is a noun 'burden/load' used figuratively, and خواست is the past infinitive/verb 'asked/wanted' — together: 'the doorkeeper ought not to have treated you as a burden / ought not to have demanded you as a charge.'
segments.s398-g140: به نزد من آی is the imperative 'come to me' (آی is the vocative/imperative form 'come!'). آنگهی is a linking adverb 'thereupon/then'. کت هواست is a nominal phrase followed by the copula ست 'is' (کت = 'coat' here is literal in the lexicon but functions idiomatically; هوا = 'desire/mood' and ست = 'is'), so syntactically this is 'then it is the desire/mood (so)' — i.e. 'then it will be as you wish.'
segments.s398-g141: ازان پس = 'after that/thereupon' (azān + pas). بپرسیدش is the polite or plural imperative 'ask him' (ب‑ prefix marks subjunctive/imperative; the -ش suffix = 'him'). از here means 'about/concerning' and introduces the topic: 'ask him about رنج راه' = 'the hardships of the road.' Word order places the topical prepositional phrase after the verb.
segments.s398-g142: This is a short series of topics introduced by the preposition ز (poetic from of از = 'about/from'): 'ز ایران و توران و کار سپاه' = 'about Iran and Turan and the affairs/work of the army.' The coordinating و simply links the items; کار سپاه is a noun phrase 'the affairs/work of the army' (کار + genitive/attributive سپاه).
segments.s398-g143: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed expression 'thus gave answer' (چنین = 'thus', داد = 'gave', پاسخ = 'answer'). که introduces the reported content ('that'); the following personal pronoun and temporal noun 'من ماه پنج' place the speaker and duration ('I [for] five months'). Note the older word order where numeral follows the counted noun.
segments.s398-g144: کشیدم is the 1st‑person past of کشیدن used here in the 'to endure/suffer' sense ('I endured'). به راه is the locative/adverbial 'on the road'; اندرون 'within' modifies the following nouns; درد و رنج is a coordinated pair 'pain and suffering.' The verb comes before the locative phrase as common in classical Persian narration.
segments.s398-g97: چنین (an adverb) precedes the verb to mean 'thus/so'; داد is a past-tense verb 'gave/said' (honorific/narrative past here) and پاسخ is the direct object 'answer/reply'. که introduces the reported content ('that' / 'that which follows'). دل شاددار is an instruction: دل 'heart' is the theme and شاددار is an adjective combined with دار to express the sense 'keep (it) joyful' — a terse imperative-like exhortation in poetic style.
segments.s398-g98: ز هر بد is a prepositional phrase 'from/out of every bad/evil' (ز = 'from'); تن خویش is 'one's body/person' with خویش split into خوی + ش (possessive); آزاد دار is parallel to شاددار: آزاد 'free' + دار giving the imperative sense 'keep [it] free'. Word order is prepositional phrase → noun phrase → imperative verb (poetic compression).
segments.s398-g99: نیازاردت is a poetic/jussive verb form expressing a wish or negative potential toward the addressee — here best rendered 'may no one afflict/harm you' (the verb carries the object clitic -ت for 'you'). کس is the indefinite object 'anyone/someone'. به توران زمین is a locative phrase 'in/on the land of Turan' (به + place + زمین). The clause therefore means 'may no one trouble you in the land of Turan.'
segments.s398-g100: همان‌گر is a conjunctive/conditional adverb 'if likewise/just so'; گرایی is a nominal form meaning 'inclination/way' (i.e. 'if you follow the same way'). به introduces the destinations; ماچین و چین names distant lands. The clause expresses a conditional continuation: 'if you do the same, (you will go) to Machin and China.'
segments.s398-g101: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past tense verb 'he commanded/said'; پس is a temporal/conclusive adverb 'then'. تا here introduces the content/purpose clause ('that ...') — 'so that / in order that' — and سرای فراخ is the noun phrase 'a spacious house/dwelling' (noun + adjective; Persian places the adjective after the noun).
segments.s398-g102: به دژ بر is a locative direction 'to/at the fortress, on/against'; یکی here means 'one / a single / a certain' (used as an indefinite numeral/adjective); کلبه is 'cottage' and در پیش کاخ is the locative phrase 'in front of the palace' (در پیش = 'before/in front of' + کاخ 'palace'). Word order groups the modifiers after the noun as is typical in Persian poetry.
segments.s398-g103: به رویین دژاندر is a locative phrase 'in/within the iron citadel' (رویین 'iron(-hard)' modifies دژ 'fortress'); مر is the indirect-object clitic 'to me' and او را is the direct object 'him' with the object marker را. دهند is 3rd-person-plural present/subjunctive used narratively 'they give' — together the phrase means 'in the iron citadel they give him to me.'
segments.s398-g104: همه بارش 'all his burden' (همه = all; بار = load/burden; ش = his). از دشت marks the source 'from the plain' and بر سر نهند is a verb phrase 'they place upon (his) head' (بر سر = 'upon the head'; نهند = 3rd-plural verbal form 'they place/set' — here used narratively). The whole clause says they heap/put all his burden from the plain onto his head.
segments.s398-g153: که here is a relative/connecting particle 'who/that' referring back to the person just mentioned; رزم آزماید is a present/habitual-type verb phrase (آزماید from آزمایشیدن/آزموختن) meaning 'tests/tries battle' or 'proves himself in battle'. به + توران زمین is a prepositional phrase 'into/onto the land of Turan' (توران modified by زمین = 'Turan-land'), with به (towards/into) marking direction.
segments.s398-g154: بخواهد is a modal/volitional present ('wants/has the will') expressing desire or intention. به مردی ز ارجاسپ کین is the object of that desire: به مردی = 'against / upon a man' (indefinite 'a man'), ز ارجاسپ = 'of/from Arjasp' (i.e. a man belonging to Arjasp), and کین = 'vengeance' (the thing he desires). The clause therefore means 'he wishes vengeance on a man of Arjasp.'
segments.s398-g155: بخندید is a past tense verb 'he laughed'. The following ارجاسپ گفت places the speaker's name with گفت ('said'); the sequence is narrative: 'Arjasp laughed and said these words' (این سخن = 'these words' / 'this speech'), with گفت introducing the quoted content.
segments.s398-g156: نگوید here is negative present (classical literary form) meaning 'does not say' (3rd‑person), placed before the subject by poetic inversion. The subject follows: جهاندیده مرد کهن = 'a world‑wise (experienced), old man.' Together the clause reads 'a world‑wise, old man would not say such words' (i.e. 'such words are not befitting an experienced old man').
segments.s398-g157: اگر is the conditional 'if'. کرگس آید is a present/future‑oriented clause 'a vulture comes' (آید = 'comes' / 'should come'). سوی هفتخوان is a directional phrase 'toward the Haft‑Khwān / the Seven Trials' (سوی = toward(s)). The whole clause sets the conditional scene: 'If a vulture comes to the Seven Trials...'.
segments.s398-g158: مرا (here split as م + را in the word list) is the accusative 'me'. اهرمن خوان is an imperative: خوان (from خواندن used poetically) = 'call/name', so 'call me Ahriman'. و links the two commands. مردم مخوان is a negative imperative 'do not call (me) a man/among men' — مخوان here functions as the negative form of خواندن in this poetic context. Overall: 'call me Ahriman and do not call me (a) man.'
segments.s398-g159: چو is a temporal 'when'. بشنید is past 'he heard'. جنگی زمین = 'a battle on earth' (جنگی qualifying زمین), and بوسه داد is a common collocation 'gave a kiss' (= 'he kissed' — here a gesture made on hearing the news). Word order is poetic: temporal clause + what was heard + the reaction 'bوسه داد'.
segments.s398-g160: بیامد is past 'he came'. ز ایوان is the source/place 'from the iwan/portico'. ارجاسپ is the subject name and شاد is a predicate/adjective 'joyful' placed after the name: 'Arjasp, joyful, came out of the iwan.' The sentence closes the narrative action begun in the prior clause.
segments.s398-g105: بسازد is the classical narrative verb 'to build/make' (b‑ + سازد); in epic Persian this present form is used to report a past action ('he built'). بران is a fused prepositional phrase (بر + آن = 'on/thereon'), placed after the verb in this poetic word order. کلبه بازارگاه is a noun phrase ('a cottage/booth of the marketplace') with بازارگاه as postmodifier.
segments.s398-g106: همی is a habitual/continuous adverb (here: 'keeps on / always') placed before the verb to mark ongoing action. داردش is دارد + enclitic ‑ش ('has/keeps it'); the object pronoun is attached to the verb. ایمن اندر پناه is a predicative locative: ایمن ('safe') + اندر ('in/within') + پناه ('refuge/protection'), i.e. 'keeps it safe in (his) shelter.'
segments.s398-g107: برفتند is the simple past 3rd‑person plural 'they went/off departed.' The conjunction و links the clauses. صندوقها را uses the plural صندوقها with the definite object marker را ('the chests' as direct object). به پشت is a locative/instrumental phrase 'onto/onto the back' (i.e. 'on their backs').
segments.s398-g108: کشیدند is the past tense 'they pulled / they hauled.' The following noun phrase 'ماهار اشتر' is a genitive construction 'the bridle of Ashtar' (owner after the thing). به مشت is an instrumental/locative phrase 'in/with the fist,' indicating how they held the bridle.
segments.s398-g109: یکی مرد بخرد is a nominal phrase 'a certain/one wise man' (یکی = 'a certain/someone', مرد = 'man', بخرد = adjective 'wise'). بپرسید is the past/past‑narrative form 'he asked' and گفت follows as the reporting verb 'and said.' The sequence is typical: subject phrase → verb(s).
segments.s398-g110: که introduces the content clause (here an indirect question) 'that/what...'. صندوق را is the definite object 'the chest' (را marks definiteness/object). چیست is the archaic copular interrogative 'what is' (چی + ست). اندر نهفت is a post‑verbal locative + participle: اندر = 'in/within', نهفت = 'hidden/hidden away', yielding 'what is hidden inside the chest.'
segments.s398-g111: کشنده here is a participial noun from کشیدن meaning 'the one who pulls' — i.e. 'the carrier/porter' (not literally 'killer' in this context). بدو = به + او 'to him.' گفت is the reporting past tense 'said.' ما هوش خویش is 'we + our هوش' where هوش literally means 'mind/awareness' (here contextually 'our (own) concern/safety/possessions') and خویش is the reflexive/possessive ('our/one's own') formed from خوی + enclitic ش.
segments.s398-g112: نهادیم is the 1st‑person plural past 'we placed/put' (نهاد + ‑یم). ناچار functions adverbially here 'necessarily / compelled to / inevitably.' بر دوش خویش is a locative/possessive phrase: بر = 'on/upon', دوش = 'shoulder', خویش = 'our own' (خوی + enclitic ش), so 'upon our own shoulders.'
segments.s398-g113: Word order is object (one/certain cottage) + verb + agent: 'یکی کلبه برساخت اسفندیار' literally places 'یکی کلبه' ('a certain/a single cottage') before the verb 'برساخت' (past 'built') and then gives the agent 'اسفندیار' at the end; this is a common poetic ordering. 'یکی' here is an indefinite numeral/adjective modifying 'کلبه' (= 'a certain / one cottage').
segments.s398-g114: 'بیاراست' is an archaic/simple past form meaning 'he adorned / made beautiful'. It governs the following simile 'همچون گل اندر بهار' ('like a flower in spring'), where 'همچون' introduces the comparison, 'گل' is the image ('flower'), and 'اندر بهار' is a locative/time phrase ('in/among spring').
segments.s398-g115: 'ز هر سو' is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from every side/direction'; 'فراوان' is an adverb/adjective meaning 'in great number(s)'; the noun 'خریدار' (buyer) is plural/collective by sense; 'خاست' is the archaic past of خاستن ('arose', 'appeared'). The clause literally: 'From every side plentiful buyers arose.'
segments.s398-g116: The demonstrative/adverb 'بران' ('upon that / thereon') refers back to the cottage. Word order 'بران کلبه بر تیز بازار خاست' places the locative/target ('بران کلبه' = 'around/on that cottage') before 'بر' + location 'تیز بازار' and the verb 'خاست' ('sprang up'). 'تیز بازار' is a poetic description of the market as brisk/lively (an archaic adjective-noun pairing).
segments.s398-g117: 'ببود' is an archaic past copula/verb 'was' used here to set time: 'ببود آن شب' = 'that night (it was)' and linked by 'و' to another time expression 'بامداد پگاه' ('the break of morning / early dawn'). The two time-phrases together indicate the span 'that night and at dawn.'
segments.s398-g118: The verb phrase 'دوان شد' is a light-verb/periphrastic past (literally 'became running') meaning 'ran / rushed'. 'ز ایوان' marks the source ('from the iwan/porch'); 'به نزدیک شاه' marks the goal ('toward/near the king'). Word order places source before the verb and goal after the auxiliary 'شد'.
segments.s398-g119: 'ز' at the start functions as an ablative/partitive 'of/from' introducing the items being listed. The sequence lists gifts: 'دینار' (money/coins), then 'وز مشک' (here the token 'وز' appears; in context this functions as a connective but is not one of the provided senses), then 'مشک' (musk), 'و دیبا' (and brocade), concluding with 'تخت' (throne/bed/couch) modified by the numeral 'سه' in the surface text. This is a poetic catalog of goods; word order groups the materials before naming the furniture.
segments.s398-g120: 'همی' is the continuous/iterative particle often used in classical Persian to mark a habitual or ongoing past action ('kept on / did repeatedly' or simply past narrative 'he then'). Here it combines with the verb 'برد' (a past tense 'took/carried') to mean 'he carried (forth)'. 'پیش اندرون نیکبخت' places the destination ('before/into the inner rooms of the fortunate one') after the verb. Note: the lemma senses given for 'برد' in the data do not list the verbal sense 'he carried', so that word is marked accordingly in the senses map.
segments.s398-g129: 'اگر' introduces a conditional ('if'); 'هیچ' here functions as an indefinite 'any' (poetic/archaic use) modifying 'شایسته' ('worthy'). 'بیند' is 3rd-person singular present used for potential/subjunctive ('he finds' / 'should he find'); the prepositional phrase 'به گنج' ('in/to the treasure/treasury') follows the verb in this poetic word order.
segments.s398-g130: The verb 'بیارد' here functions as a literary subjunctive/optative ('bring' / 'should bring'); 'همانا' is an emphatic adverb 'truly/indeed'. 'ندارد' is the present negative 'does not have' (possessive/necessity), and 'به رنج' ('to/for toil') expresses the thing he lacks—i.e. 'has no need of toil'. Note the omission of an explicit auxiliary like 'نیاز' is typical in poetic diction.
segments.s398-g131: 'پذیرفتن' is the infinitive/masdar 'to accept' or 'receiving' and here forms a noun-phrase; 'از' marks source ('from'); 'شهریار زمین' is a compound 'the king/sovereign of the land' (شهریار = king, زمین = land/country). Together: 'to receive from the king of the land'.
segments.s398-g132: The poetic preposition 'ز' = 'from' introduces the human source 'بازارگان' ('the merchants'). 'پوزش و آفرین' are two coordinated nouns ('pardon/excuse' and 'acclamation/bravo' — here functioning as things received); 'و' links them. This phrase completes what is being accepted/received.
segments.s398-g133: 'بخندید' is the past simple 'he laughed' (3rd sing.). The proper name 'ارجاسپ' is the subject; poetic word order places verb before subject. 'بنواختش' is a past tense verb ('he struck/played it') with the clitic object '-ش' ('it'); the two verbs are joined by 'و'.
segments.s398-g134: 'گرانمایه‌تر' is the comparative adjective 'more precious' (the -تر comparative suffix); 'پایگه' = 'base/pedestal/foundation' is the noun being described, and 'ساختش' ('he made/built it') is the past-tense verb with the object clitic '-ش' referring back to that base. The clause means he caused the pedestal to become more valuable.
segments.s398-g135: 'چه' is the interrogative 'what'; the phrase 'چه نامی' functions as 'what name'. 'بدو' is the dative clitic 'to him'; 'گفت' is the past tense 'said' (verb); 'خراد' is the speaker's name (subject). Word order is poetic: object/question phrase + indirect object + verb + subject. The final 'نام' repeats/marks 'name' as the noun concerned.
segments.s398-g136: 'جهانجوی' is an epithet meaning 'world-seeking' / 'ambitious one' applied as the name/description given; 'با' = 'with' links the following epithets 'رادی و شادکام' ('generous/magnanimous and fortunate'), which further describe the person. The whole segment is an appositive phrase specifying the name and qualities attributed.
segments.s398-g121: بیامد and ببوسید are two past finite verbs placed in sequence to narrate successive actions ('he came' then 'he kissed'). ببوسید is 3rd person singular past; روی زمین is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'on the ground' that follows the verb. The sequence is typical of epic diction: V V + locative.
segments.s398-g122: بر here functions as a directional/prepositional marker 'to/against/toward' introducing the indirect object (ارجاسپ). چندی is an adverb 'for a while'. بکرد is an archaic past form 'he made/did' and here takes آفرین ('praise/"bravo"') as its object: 'he bestowed/spew out praise (to Arjasp)'.
segments.s398-g123: چنین گفت is a fixed narrational phrase 'thus he said'. کاین is the fused classical demonstrative (که + این) 'that/this' pointing to the following noun. مایه‌ور is an adjectival compound 'rich/wealthy' modifying کاروان; word order is demonstrative + adjective + noun.
segments.s398-g124: همی here is the old epic aspectual particle giving a continuous or prospective sense (often rendering English 'will' or 'shall' in reported speech). راندم is the verb 'to drive' (classical form), which together with همی yields 'I/ he will drive' in this reported utterance. تیز is an adverb 'swift(ly)'; با + ساروان = 'with the caravan‑leaders/ drivers'. The subject is omitted as typical in Persian reported speech.
segments.s398-g125: بدو is a dative prepositional pronoun 'to/with him'. اندرون is a locative 'inside/within' (here 'in his inner quarters'). یاره is an attendant/follower; افسرست is a nominal + copula-like formation (افسر + -ست) meaning 'is an officer/there is an officer' (ست = 'is'). The whole phrase states existence/possession: 'with him, inside, was an attendant and an officer.'
segments.s398-g126: که introduces a relative clause 'who/which'. شاه سرافراز را is the definite noun phrase 'the illustrious king' marked with را (the direct/definite marker) — here it is the reference for 'befitting'. در خورست is an idiomatic classical predicate meaning 'is fitting/befitting' (here: 'who was befitting the king'); خورست is an archaic predicative form (not the literal 'is eaten' in this context).
segments.s398-g127: بگوید is a reported-speech form meaning 'he said/told' (classical narrative use; the provided sense list lacks the spoken‑verb sense). به گنجور 'to the treasurer/treasury' (here a dative: give orders to the treasurer). تا introduces purpose 'so that/in order that' and خواسته is a noun/adjective 'that which is wanted / the desired [things]'. Together: 'he told the treasurer so that (he) should see/bring what was desired.'
segments.s398-g128: ببیند is a subjunctive/purposive form 'may see / should see' governed by تا (expressing purpose). همه 'all/everything' modifies کلبه 'the cottage/room'; آراسته is a past participial/adjectival 'adorned/arrayed'. Word order here is verb (purpose) + full NP complement describing the state of the room.
segments.s398-g81: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning 'when'. دیدش is the past transitive verb 'he saw it' (verb + object clitic -ش). فرو ریخت is a compound verbal phrase (فرو- prefix + ریخت 'poured/was poured') meaning 'poured out/scattered'; دینار is its direct object. و گفت 'and said' links the action to the quoted content that follows (the reported speech introduced by که in the next segment).
segments.s398-g82: که introduces reported speech ('that' / 'that he said ...'). با is the preposition 'with'. شهریاران is the plural noun 'rulers/kings'. خرد (here a noun) stands as 'wisdom' or adjectival idea 'the wise' modifying شهریاران. باد is the optative particle 'may' (classical/poetic usage placed after the noun), and جفت means 'pair' or 'be paired with'. Together the clause reads roughly 'that (they) may be paired with the wise rulers.'
segments.s398-g83: یکی is the indefinite 'a certain' / 'one'. مردم‌ای (here formed from مردم) names the social group 'a man of the people' or 'one among the folk' (poetic expression). شاه بازارگان is an appositional/title phrase 'king/chief of merchants' or 'master among merchants' (شاه combined with بازارگان). The phrase introduces a particular person by social label.
segments.s398-g84: This is a nominal clause with omitted copula (common in classical poetry): پدر ترک 'a father (was) a Turk' and مادر ز آزادگان 'and a mother from the free ones'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. و simply coordinates the two nominal statements ('father ... and mother ...').
segments.s398-g85: ز + place (توران) gives the origin 'from Turan'; به introduces destinations ('to خرم to ایران'). برم is a first-person form meaning 'I go / I will go' in classical Persian (here expressing the speaker's movement). The clause lists origin and destinations in sequence; the finite verb appears at the end as normal in Persian.
segments.s398-g86: وگر is the conditional 'and if / or if' introducing an alternative. سوی is the preposition 'toward'; دشت دلیران is a genitive construction 'the plain of the brave'. برم again is 'I go' placed at the end; the clause therefore means 'or if (I) go toward the plain of the brave.'
segments.s398-g87: یکی 'a certain / one'. کاروانی شتر is a noun phrase 'a caravan (of) camels' (adjective-like use of کاروانی with شتر). با منست is an archaic/poetic fused form: با + من است → 'is with me' / 'accompanies me' (classical contraction), so the whole segment says 'a caravan of camels is with me.'
segments.s398-g88: ز introduces what the caravan carries: پوشیدنی جامه‌های 'garments for wearing' (پوشیدنی = to be worn; جامه‌های = garments). نشست here is used poetically to indicate that the garments are placed/loaded (literally 'sat' / 'were set'), i.e. the caravan is laden with those clothes. The clause is elliptical and descriptive of the caravan's cargo.
segments.s398-g57: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. بانگ is a noun 'cry/sound' and درای here qualifies that cry (literally 'the cry of the wealthy/possessing'); آمد is the simple past 'came'. از introduces the source 'from' and کاروان is the source noun 'caravan.' The whole unit gives a temporal clause 'When a cry came from the caravan...'.
segments.s398-g58: همی is the archaic/poetic imperfective/intensifying particle used with past verbs (here rendering a continuous or narrative past). رفت is the past of 'go'. پیش functions adverbially 'forward/ahead', اندرون means 'inside/interior', and ساروان is 'caravan-leader' (the agent who goes). The clause describes the leader moving forward into the inner area.
segments.s398-g59: به دژ gives a locative/directional phrase 'to/at the fortress' (به here used for location/toward). نامدارن is a plural noun 'the renowned ones' modifying the implied subject; خبر is the object 'news', and یافتند is the past plural 'they found/received'. Together: 'the renowned men at the fortress received the news.'
segments.s398-g60: فراوان is an adverb 'plentifully/widely' modifying the verb that follows. بگفتند is the simple past plural 'they said/announced' (the b- is the older narrative/past marker), و is the coordinating 'and', and بشتافتند is past plural 'they hurried/rushed'. The clause therefore reports they spread the news widely and hurried off.
segments.s398-g61: که here introduces a reporting/relative clause 'that/there' (common in narrative: 'that there came...'). آمد is past 'came'. یکی is the indefinite 'a certain', مرد 'man', and بازارگان 'merchant'—together 'there came a certain merchant.'
segments.s398-g62: درمگان is 'the moneyed / coin‑holders' and فرو ... شد is a verbal phrase 'fell/plunged (down) / became' with فرو as an adverbial intensifier 'deeply/completely' and شد the past of 'become'. به before دینارگان indicates movement into a class/status 'into the dinar‑holders'. The combined sense is he plunged into the ranks of the rich—i.e., became one of the dinar‑holders.
segments.s398-g63: بزرگان is the plural subject 'the great ones' and دژ specifies their place 'of the fortress' (classical syntax omits ezāfe). پیش means 'forward/in front', باز is 'again' (or 'back/forward' in context), and آمدند is the past plural 'they came.' So: 'the great men of the fortress came forward again.'
segments.s398-g64: This is a second clause with two coordinated nouns: خریدار 'buyers' and گردن‌فراز 'those with raised necks' (an idiom for 'proud/haughty'), followed by آمدند 'they came'. Grammatically these nouns are either appositive descriptors of the arriving men or a separate nominative clause: 'buyers and the proud came.'
segments.s398-g65: This is a compact past-tense clause: 'بپرسید' (asked) is the past 3rd-person plural verb; the subject is implied (they). 'هریک' (each one) follows the verb as its logical object/subject-marker in poetry. 'ز سالار بار' combines the preposition 'ز' (from/of) with the compound noun 'سالار بار' — literally 'master of the load', i.e. the caravan leader in charge of the cargo. Word order (verb — subject/phrase) is poetic but normal in older/epic Persian.
segments.s398-g66: 'کزین بارها' is a prepositional phrase: 'کزین' (from this/these) + 'بارها' (the loads, plural). 'چیست' is the fused interrogative/copula 'what is'. The clause ends with 'کاید به کار', an idiomatic, somewhat archaic expression meaning 'what use/what will it avail' — here functioning as a question about the purpose or usefulness of the cargo. Grammatically, the relative/interrogative 'چیست' governs the following predicate 'کاید به کار'.
segments.s398-g67: 'چنین داد پاسخ' is 'he gave such an answer' (چنین = thus/so; داد = gave; پاسخ = answer). The following 'که' is a complementizer introducing the content of that answer. 'باری نخست' is an adverbial phrase meaning 'first of all' or 'first'; 'باری' here has the sense 'once/at first' rather than 'load'.
segments.s398-g68: 'به تن شاه' is an idiomatic prepositional phrase meaning 'to the king himself' (به + تن used adverbially = in person). 'باید' is the impersonal/modal 'it is necessary/one must'. The complementizer 'که' introduces the subordinate clause with the speaker’s obligation: 'بینم' is the present subjunctive/indicative first-person 'I see' (here 'that I should see'), and 'درست' modifies 'بینم' meaning 'properly/rightly' — so the clause reads 'it is necessary that I see the king in person properly/for a proper judgment.'
segments.s398-g69: 'توانایی خویش پیدا کنم' is a first-person clause: 'توانایی' (ability) + 'خویش' (his/one's own — here split as 'خوی' + enclitic 'ش') is the possessive, and 'پیدا کنم' is a verbal phrase 'I will find/discover'. Grammatically, 'پیدا کنم' is present-subjunctive/intentional (I will/shall find), so the whole means 'I will discover my own ability.'
segments.s398-g70: 'چو فرمان دهد' is a temporal subordinate clause: 'چو' = 'when', 'فرمان' = 'the command/order', and 'دهد' = 'he gives' (3rd singular). The result clause 'دیده دریا کنم' uses a poetic, slightly compressed word-order: 'دیده' (eye/sight) + 'دریا' (sea) + 'کنم' (I will make/do) literally 'I will make my eye the sea' — idiomatically 'I will turn my gaze to the sea' or 'I will set out to the sea.' The verb 'کنم' is first-person present-future intent.
segments.s398-g71: 'شتربار بنهاد' is a past action: 'شتربار' (camel-load/cargo) + 'بنهاد' (he placed/put down — past of نهادن). The conjunction 'و' links to 'خود رفت پیش' where 'خود' (himself) is emphatic and 'رفت' is the past tense 'he went', 'پیش' = 'forward/ahead'. The clause describes sequential actions: he unloaded the camel and then himself went on ahead.
segments.s398-g72: This is a purpose/result clause introduced by 'که تا' (که + تا used here 'so that/in order that'). 'چون کند' = 'how he might do' or 'what he should do' ('چون' = how, 'کند' = he does/might do). 'تیز بازار خویش' is a compressed NP: 'تیز' (swift/keen; adverbial here = 'quickly' or 'brisk') + 'بازار' (market/trade) + the possessive suffix split as 'خوی' + 'ش' (his own). Together: 'so that he might quicken/hasten his own market/trade.'
segments.s398-g9: توایدر is a fused vocative + preposition cluster (تو ای در...), addressing someone: “O you, in…”. شب و روز is a standard adverbial phrase “night and day.” بیدار باش is an imperative: بیدار (adjective 'awake/alert') + باش (imperative auxiliary 'be!'), so together “be awake/keep watch.” Word order is emphatic (vocative first, then time phrase, then the imperative).
segments.s398-g10: سپه را uses the object marker را to mark 'the army' as definite (here the thing to be guarded). ز دشمن is the poetic preposition 'from' (ز) plus 'enemy'. نگهدار باش is a periphrastic command: نگهدار here functions as a noun/agent ('guardian, preserver') used with باش to mean 'be a guardian' or more simply 'guard.' The whole segment is an imperative: 'guard the army from the enemy.'
segments.s398-g11: تن here means 'person/self' rather than just 'body' (poetic for 'man'). آنگه = 'then'/'in that case.' شود is the present/future-tense verb 'becomes/will be.' بی‌گمان is an adverb 'undoubtedly.' ارجمند is a predicative adjective 'esteemed/valued.' Together: 'The man will then, undoubtedly, become esteemed.'
segments.s398-g12: سزاوار is an adjective meaning 'deserving/fit (for)'; here it takes the following nouns as its objects without an explicit ezāfe (a typical poetic omission): 'سزاوار شاهی و تخت بلند' = 'deserving of kingship and a high/lofty throne.' شاهی is the noun 'kingship' (not the adjectival sense), تخت 'throne', بلند 'high/lofty.' This segment completes the predicate begun in the previous clause.
segments.s398-g13: کز is a relative 'who/that.' انبوه دشمن = 'a multitude/host of enemies.' نترسد is the present negative of ترسیدن 'not fear / will not be afraid'; it governs the following prepositional complement به جنگ 'into battle' (به here means 'to/into' or 'in (battle)'). Grammatically this clause describes someone who does not fear fighting even against overwhelming numbers.
segments.s398-g14: This is a series of similes/adverbial loci linked to نترسد: 'به کوه از پلنگ' = 'on the mountain (as) of/like the leopard' and 'به آب از نهنگ' = 'in the water (as) of/like the whale.' The preposition به marks the domain (to/in), and از here functions comparatively ('of/like' in poetic usage). The clause paints bravery in both mountain and sea with animal comparisons.
segments.s398-g15: به جایی here functions adverbially 'in one place / at some places.' فریب is a noun 'deception/guile.' The parallel 'و به جایی نهیب' repeats the same pattern: 'and in another place a cry/threat' — نهیب has the sense of a loud cry or thunderous call (a threatening shout). The verbs are omitted (imperative or hortatory sense is implied by the preceding commands): 'do this here, that there.'
segments.s398-g16: گهی ... گهی = 'sometimes ... sometimes' (adverb of frequency). فر 'farr/royal glory' and زیب 'beauty/adornment' are abstract nouns describing times of splendour; در نشیب 'in decline/descent' uses در + نشیب to mean 'in a low point/decline.' The clause is verbless (the verb 'be' is implied), continuing the parallelism of states: sometimes splendour and beauty, sometimes decline.
segments.s398-g41: بیاورد is the literary 3rd-person-singular past tense meaning 'he brought/placed'. The object صندوق ('chest') follows the verb and is quantified by the postposed numeral phrase هشتاد جفت ('eighty pairs'), a classical word order where the number follows the noun or directly follows it as a classifier.
segments.s398-g42: همه modifies بند صندوقها = 'all the straps/fastenings of the chests.' در is the preposition 'in/inside' and نهفت here is a past (literary) verb meaning 'hid/concealed.' Together they mean 'he concealed (them) inside' with the direct object supplied by 'همه بند صندوقها.'
segments.s398-g43: صد و شست is the compound numeral 'one hundred and sixty' (here written in older form). The phrase places the numeral with the noun مرد ('men'); از یلان is a prepositional phrase 'from the heroes/warriors.' برگزید is the 3rd-person-singular past 'chose/selected.'
segments.s398-g44: کزیشان is a contracted relative/compound 'که از ایشان' meaning 'from whom/of whom.' نهانش = نهان + -ش is 'his/their hidden (thing)' or 'his concealment' (the possessive clitic refers back to the preceding referent). نیاید پدید is a fixed classical sequence meaning 'would not come to light/appear' (نیاید 'may not come/does not come' + پدید 'appear'), expressing that the concealment would not be revealed by them.
segments.s398-g45: تنی here means 'a certain number/party of' and pairs with the numeral بیست ('twenty') to indicate 'twenty (persons).' از نامداران خویش is 'from his renowned ones': از = 'from,' نامداران = 'renowned men,' and خویش + ش is the possessive idea 'his own.' The phrase names which twenty are meant.
segments.s398-g46: سرافراز is an epithet/adjective 'honored/proud,' coordinated with خنجرگزاران ('dagger-bearers,' a plural agent noun) by و 'and.' خویش + -ش again marks possession 'his,' so the two coordinated terms further describe those twenty men (they are his proud ones and his dagger-bearers).
segments.s398-g47: بفرمود is the honorific/literary past 'he commanded/said.' تا introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/result ('so that' / 'that'), and بر سر کاروان is a prepositional phrase 'at the head of the caravan' (بر = 'on/upon,' سر = 'head/chief,' کاروان = 'caravan'). This phrase functions as the locative complement to the verb in the subordinate clause.
segments.s398-g48: بوند is the older/literary 3rd-person-plural copula/verb 'they should be / they were' here used after تا to express the commanded/resultant state 'that they be.' آن is the demonstrative 'those/the,' گرانمایگان is an honorific plural 'the illustrious/esteemed ones,' and ساروان names their office 'caravan‑leaders/camel‑drivers.' Together they form the subject/predicate of the subordinate clause.
segments.s398-g49: This is a terse, verbless description listing garments: subject is understood (’they’/’there were’). The prepositional phrase به پای اندرون literally 'at/on the feet (in)side' says where the کفش (shoes) were, and در تن (lit. 'in/on the body') introduces گلیم as worn like a covering. Word order is noun phrases without an explicit copula, typical of poetic Persian ellipsis.
segments.s398-g50: Another nominal clause with an omitted verb: به بار اندرون 'in the inner load/baggage' introduces the inventory, followed by an asyndetic list گوهر و زر و سیم (gems, gold, and silver). The initial به marks location/containment and the whole phrase functions like 'there were'.
segments.s398-g51: سپهبد is the subject; به دژ 'toward the fortress' gives direction. روی بنهاد is the idiom 'put on a (certain) face/expression' (بنهاد = past of نهادن 'to place/assume'), and تفت here describes that expression as scorching/ferocious (poetic use). The clause is compact and uses the past narrative tense.
segments.s398-g52: به کردار بازارگانان 'in the manner/guise of merchants' is an adverbial phrase modifying the following verb. برفت is an archaic past form meaning 'he went' (see explanation); together the phrase means 'he proceeded, behaving like a merchant.'
segments.s398-g53: همی is a particle indicating continued or habitual action ('still/kept on'), and راند is the past verb 'drove/led'. با نامور کاروان is an instrumental/associative phrase 'with the famed caravan'—together 'kept leading the famous caravan.'
segments.s398-g54: This is a nominal simile: یلان سرافراز 'heroes, lofty/proud' followed by چون ساروان 'like caravan‑leaders'. The copula 'were' is omitted (typical in Persian descriptive lines), so the phrase reads 'the proud heroes were like caravan chiefs.'
segments.s398-g55: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. نزدیک دژ شد is a light-verb construction meaning 'became/grew near the fortress' (i.e. 'drew near'). برفت is an archaic past form meaning 'he went/advanced'; ز پیش 'from the front' indicates position/direction. The sequence of verbs is narrative: upon nearing, he advanced.
segments.s398-g56: بدید is past 'he saw'. آن دل و رای 'that heart and counsel' is a coordinated object (دل = mind/resolve, رای = plan/counsel) qualified by هشیار 'alert/vigilant'. خویش is the possessive 'his' split into خوی + ش in the manuscript/lexicon; together they mean 'his own.' The clause reports an internal perception of his heart and prudent plan.
segments.s398-g17: چو introduces a conditional ('if') here. بازارگانی is used adverbially/nominally ('as a trader, in the manner of merchants'). بدین is the prefixed form built with ب- (here the prefix 'be-/to-') plus the demonstrative/particle, governing the following noun دژ ('at/to this'), and شوم is the first‑person present/subjunctive form of شدن ('I become') used in the protasis of the conditional.
segments.s398-g18: نگویم is a negative present/subjunctive or jussive form of گفتن meaning 'I do not say / I will not say.' که is the subordinating conjunction 'that' introducing the reported clause. شیر جهان پهلوم is a noun phrase 'the lion of the world (شیر جهان) at my side (پهلم)', with پهلوم the locative/possessive 'my side/at my flank.' The clause as a whole is the apodosis: 'I will not say that ...'.
segments.s398-g19: فراز آورم is a verb phrase with فراز ('up, aloft, forth') plus آورم (first‑person present/subjunctive of آوردن) meaning 'I bring up / I will bring.' چاره is the direct object 'a remedy/solution.' از هر دری is a prepositional phrase 'from every door/source'; دری here means 'door/source' or 'avenue' (a poetic plural sense), though the supplied senses list does not include that sense.
segments.s398-g20: بخوانم is the first‑person present/subjunctive of خواندن and here means 'I will read / I will call forth.' ز (poetic of از) marks source: 'from.' هر دانشی = 'every kind/branch of knowledge' (دانش = knowledge). دفتری is used as a noun meaning 'a book/volume/folio' (from دفتر), but the supplied senses list gives only office‑related senses; here it functions as a literary 'book/volume.'
segments.s398-g21: تو is the vocative/subject 'you.' بی‌دیده‌بان is a predicative adjective 'without a watch / unguarded / lacking a sentinel.' و links the two predicates, طلایه is another predicate meaning 'vanguard/forefront.' مباش is the prohibitive second‑person singular of بودن ('do not be'). The clause is an admonition: 'Do not be unguarded and do not be the vanguard.'
segments.s398-g22: ز هر دانشی is 'from every kind of knowledge' (ز = poetic از). سست مایه is a compound nominal phrase: سست 'weak' modifies مایه 'source/substance/resource'; the instruction is negative and finished by مباش 'do not be' — 'do not be a weak resource/source from any knowledge.'
segments.s398-g23: اگر is the conditional 'if.' دیده‌بان is the subject 'the sentinel/watchman.' دود is the direct object 'smoke.' بیند is the present subjunctive/indicative of دیدن used here as 'sees.' به‌روز is an adverbial phrase 'by day / in daylight' (به‌روز = of the day). Together they form the protasis: 'If the sentinel sees smoke by day...'.
segments.s398-g24: شب is an adverbial time marker 'at night.' آتش is subject 'the fire.' چو is a simile particle 'like/as.' خورشید is 'the sun.' گیتی فروز is a two‑word predicate: گیتی 'the world' and فروز (a poetic verb/adjective from فروزیدن) 'illuminates / lights up' — 'the fire lights the world like the sun.' The clause is the apodosis describing the night consequence.
segments.s398-g1: وز انجا بیامد به پرده‌سرای is a compact clause in archaic/poetic word order: وز (an archaic/poetic connective meaning roughly “and/from there”) + انجا (“there/thence”) + بیامد (past tense ‘came’) + به (preposition ‘to/into’) + پرده‌سرای (the destination noun ‘pavilion/curtain‑hall’). The verb بیامد (past) follows the place expressions, which is typical in older Persian narrative style.
segments.s398-g2: ز بیگانه پردخت کردند جای is another short clause whose verb (کردند, ‘they did/made’) comes before its object/place complement. ز (poetic preposition ‘from/of’) introduces the beneficiary/related party (بیگانه, ‘the stranger’), پردخت here reflects پرداخت(ن) in an older spelling meaning ‘payment/giving’ or ‘making/assigning’, and جای (‘place’) is the direct object marked by the normal order where the verb has already appeared (کردند جای = ‘they made/assigned a place’).
segments.s398-g3: پشوتن بشد نزد اسفندیار: the proper name پشوتن is the subject, بشد is an archaic/periphrastic past-form meaning ‘it came to pass / he came / he arrived’, and نزد + اسفندیار means ‘into the presence of Esfandiyar’ (نزد used as ‘in the presence of’). The verb following the name is typical epic diction rather than modern simple past of رفت.
segments.s398-g4: سخن رفت هرگونه از کارزار: سخن ‘speech/talk’ is the subject, رفت ‘went’ is the intransitive verb meaning ‘went about’ or ‘was carried’, هرگونه (‘of every kind’/‘various’) qualifies the talk, and از کارزار (‘concerning/from the battle’) gives the topic. Word order places the qualification هرگونه before the prepositional topic phrase, producing the meaning ‘all sorts of talk went about concerning the battle.’
segments.s398-g5: بدو گفت جنگی چنین دژ به جنگ: بدو ‘to him’ (prepositional clitic meaning ‘said to him’), گفت here functions in the narrative as ‘said’ (an archaic/poetic usage; the printed senses list shows nouns but the element is the reporting verb), then an appositive phrase follows: جنگی چنین ‘a war such as this’ and دژ به جنگ ‘a fortress in war/battle’ (دژ qualified by the circumstance به جنگ). The clause strings the vocative/indirect object, reporting verb, and then the description of the thing spoken about in poetic order.
segments.s398-g6: به سال فراوان نیاید به چنگ: this is a temporal/circumstantial clause with به سال فراوان ‘for many years’ (به + year + فَراوان used adverbially) followed by نیاید (present/subjunctive/negative form here used as a timeless/predictive ‘will not come’ or ‘does not come’) and a concluding prepositional phrase به چنگ ‘into the grasp/hand’ (چنگ = grip/claw used metaphorically). The negation applies to the whole – it will not come into one’s grasp for many years.
segments.s398-g7: مگر خوار گیرم تن خویش را: مگر here introduces a conditional/exclusive sense ‘unless’ or ‘if not’, خوار is an adjective or substantive ‘despised/the lowly’, and گیرم is a first‑person subjunctive/present form meaning ‘I take/let me take / I may seize’. تن خویش را is the object ‘my body’ (تن + possessive خویش split across خوی + ش in the word list, and را marks the definite object). The clause therefore expresses a conditional action the speaker is willing to take (‘unless I become despised / unless I seize myself / unless I risk my body’), using older subjunctive morphology.
segments.s398-g8: یکی چاره سازم بداندیش را: یکی ‘one/a single’, چاره ‘remedy/solution’, سازم is 1st‑person present subjunctive/intentional ‘I will make/devise’, and بداندیش را ‘for the ill‑minded/ill‑wisher’ (را marks the direct object). Together it reads ‘I will devise one solution for the malcontent,’ with سازم expressing the speaker’s intent in poetic narrative style.
